title: Everyday Slang
description: The casual Brazilian Portuguese a young carioca or paulista actually uses with friends every day.
situation: Hanging out, texting, and chatting informally with friends in Brazil.

phrases.tip: The default casual greeting between friends. Literally 'and there?'
Works as a greeting and as 'okay, deal'. Literally 'beauty'. Often shortened to 'belê'
Safe everywhere, from friends to shopkeepers. Reply with 'tudo' or 'tudo bem'
Very common, especially in the northeast and among younger people
Classic Rio slang for something cool or a nice person
Casual 'thanks', also used as 'see ya'. From 'valer', to be worth it
Friendly address term for a guy, like 'mate'. Literally 'face'. 'Mano' is the same idea
Out of cash. Literally 'I'm smooth'. 'Tô' is the casual spoken form of 'estou'
Surprise or friendly protest. Literally 'what's this, dude'
Everyday exclamation of surprise. Short for 'Nossa Senhora' but no longer feels religious
Friendly and widely acceptable. 'Legal' is the go-to word for 'cool' or 'nice'
Filler check used mid-sentence, like 'you feel me?'. Literally 'are you switched on?'
Said when it's time to head out or get started. Literally 'let's go in this'
Dead tired, not literal. Playful exaggeration after a long day
'Pagar mico' is to embarrass yourself. Literally 'what a monkey'. Light and cheeky

phrases.english: One last drink for the road
phrases.tip: The final round before leaving a bar. A real cultural ritual in Brazil
